title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder
tex_block_html: A CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic if and only if it has a topological order.

tex_statement.raw:
% Claim statement file for row claim_3_2 -- "AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker it dispatches) fills the body below with the
% claim's statement(s) from the lecture notes -- statement only, no proof
% (proofs live in the sibling `claim_3_2_proof_*.tex` file).
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it render both standalone and as part of
% `main.tex`. Note: `main.tex` does NOT \subfile this statement file -- the
% sibling `_proof_` file restates the statement above the proof, so
% including both in `main.tex` would be redundant. This file exists for
% standalone reading / grep convenience.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_2 (statement)
% title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_2}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_2}

\begin{Lem}[AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}). Then $G$ is acyclic (in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}) if and only if there exists a topological order of $G$ (in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}); equivalently, $G$ is acyclic if and only if there exists a strict total order $<$ on $J \cup V$ -- i.e.\ a binary relation $<$ on $J \cup V$ that is irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous -- such that, for every $v, w \in J \cup V$, $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$ (using the parent-set notation $\Pa^G$ of def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i.). The label set $L$ plays no role on either side of the biconditional.
\end{Lem}

\end{document}

The label set $L$ plays no role on either side of the biconditional.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_2 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_2}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_2}

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Lem}[AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}). Then $G$ is acyclic (in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}) if and only if there exists a topological order of $G$ (in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}); equivalently, $G$ is acyclic if and only if there exists a strict total order $<$ on $J \cup V$ -- i.e.\ a binary relation $<$ on $J \cup V$ that is irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous -- such that, for every $v, w \in J \cup V$, $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$ (using the parent-set notation $\Pa^G$ of def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i.). The label set $L$ plays no role on either side of the biconditional.
\end{Lem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
% Proof lifted verbatim from the LN at lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex:228-244.
\begin{proof}
($\Leftarrow$)
Suppose $<$ is a topological order of $G$, i.e.\ $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$.
If there were a non-trivial directed walk $v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v$ ($n \ge 1$),
then $v_0 < v_1 < \cdots < v_n = v_0$, a contradiction.

\medskip\noindent($\Rightarrow$)
Suppose $G$ is acyclic. We construct a topological order by repeatedly selecting a node with no parents in the remaining graph.
Define a sequence $v_1, \dots, v_K$ ($K = |J \cup V|$) inductively: given $v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}$, let $R_i := (J \cup V) \sm \{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}$
and $G_i$ the subgraph of $G$ induced on $R_i$.
Since $G_i$ is acyclic (as an induced subgraph of an acyclic graph) and finite, it has a node $v_i$ with $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$
(otherwise, following parent edges would produce a directed cycle).
Choose any such $v_i$.

This gives a total order $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_K$.
If $v = v_j \in \Pa^G(w = v_i)$, then when $w$ was selected, $v$ was still in $R_i$, so $v$ would have been a parent of $w$ in $G_i$, contradicting $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$---unless $v$ was already selected, i.e.\ $j < i$. Hence $v < w$.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Lemma — AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg). Then $G$ is acyclic (in the sense of def def-acylic) if and only if there exists a topological order of $G$ (in the sense of def def-topological-order); equivalently, $G$ is acyclic if and only if there exists a strict total order $<$ on $J \cup V$ – i.e.\ a binary relation $<$ on $J \cup V$ that is irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous – such that, for every $v, w \in J \cup V$, $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$ (using the parent-set notation $\Pa^G$ of def def_3_5, item i.). The label set $L$ plays no role on either side of the biconditional.

Proof.

($\Leftarrow$) Suppose $<$ is a topological order of $G$, i.e.\ $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$. If there were a non-trivial directed walk $v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v$ ($n \ge 1$), then $v_0 < v_1 < \cdots < v_n = v_0$, a contradiction.

($\Rightarrow$) Suppose $G$ is acyclic. We construct a topological order by repeatedly selecting a node with no parents in the remaining graph. Define a sequence $v_1, \dots, v_K$ ($K = |J \cup V|$) inductively: given $v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}$, let $R_i := (J \cup V) \sm \{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}$ and $G_i$ the subgraph of $G$ induced on $R_i$. Since $G_i$ is acyclic (as an induced subgraph of an acyclic graph) and finite, it has a node $v_i$ with $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$ (otherwise, following parent edges would produce a directed cycle). Choose any such $v_i$.

This gives a total order $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_K$. If $v = v_j \in \Pa^G(w = v_i)$, then when $w$ was selected, $v$ was still in $R_i$, so $v$ would have been a parent of $w$ in $G_i$, contradicting $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$—unless $v$ was already selected, i.e.\ $j < i$. Hence $v < w$.

lean_blocks.explanation: This single `variable` line is statement-typing infrastructure rather than content. In Lean 4 a `variable` declaration auto-binds the listed implicits into every subsequent declaration in the namespace that mentions them, so the `theorem` below picks up `Node` and `[DecidableEq Node]` without restating them. The `Type*` annotation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, matching the convention inherited from `def_3_1` (`CDMG.lean`). The `[DecidableEq Node]` instance is load-bearing for the theorem signature in three separate places: through `def_3_2`'s `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance, which is what lets `v ∈ G` typecheck inside the topological-order quantifiers; through `def_3_5`'s `G.Pa w` parent-set in the parent-precedence clause of `G.IsTopologicalOrder`; and through the `Walk.IsDirectedWalk` recursion in `def_3_4` that `G.IsAcyclic` (from `def_3_6`) quantifies over. Stronger fixtures like `[Fintype Node]` are deliberately avoided at the statement level — the proof body may want them locally, but they would over-commit the signature.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-bind `Node` (universe-polymorphic) and `DecidableEq` for `Membership`, `Pa`, and `Walk.IsDirectedWalk` below.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
theorem acyclic_iff_topological_order (G : CDMG Node) :
    G.IsAcyclic ↔
      ∃ lt : Node → Node → Prop, G.IsTopologicalOrder lt
lean_blocks.explanation: This is the main lemma of the row — the biconditional naming the claim. The signature binds the CDMG `G` and asserts `G.IsAcyclic ↔ ∃ lt : Node → Node → Prop, G.IsTopologicalOrder lt`: the literal Lean rendering of "acyclic iff a topological order exists". The `↔` packages both directions into a single `Iff`, so downstream consumers project with `.mp` (acyclic $\Rightarrow$ existence — the route used by chapter-4+ factorisation / do-calculus traversals over an ADMG) or `.mpr` (existence $\Rightarrow$ acyclic — the certificate route used by causal-discovery algorithms) at zero syntactic cost. The existential binds a bare `Node → Node → Prop` rather than a Mathlib `LinearOrder` or subtype-restricted order: this matches `def_3_8`'s shape for `IsTopologicalOrder` (itself a property of an external relation) and avoids over-committing the ambient `Node` type or forcing subtype coercions at every use site. The proof body discharges the biconditional by walk-reachability plus Szpilrajn extension (forward) and a parent-precedes contradiction along a non-trivial directed walk (backward), routed through the seven `private` walk-level helpers above.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Main lemma: bundle both directions of "acyclic iff has a topological order" into a single `Iff`.
theorem acyclic_iff_topological_order (G : CDMG Node) :
    -- LHS: `G.IsAcyclic` from `def_3_6` — no non-trivial directed walk from any `v ∈ G` back to itself.
    G.IsAcyclic ↔
    -- RHS: existence of *some* relation `lt` on `Node` qualifying as a topological order of `G` per `def_3_8`.
      ∃ lt : Node → Node → Prop, G.IsTopologicalOrder lt

design_choices:
**Statement context `Node : Type*` with `[DecidableEq Node]` bound by `variable`.** Inherited from `def_3_1`. Both fixtures are load-bearing: `Finset`-backed membership tests on `G.J ∪ G.V`, the `v ∈ G` quantifier via `def_3_2`'s `Membership` instance, the `Walk G u v` recursion of `def_3_4`, the `IsDirectedWalk` predicate from `def_3_6`, and the `G.Pa w` construction from `def_3_5` all reach through `[DecidableEq Node]`. A three-dash helper marker wraps the typing infrastructure; the two-dash statement marker wraps only the theorem. The seven `private` walk-level helpers (concatenation, length additivity, directedness preservation, source / target membership, single-edge directed walk, walk-induced `lt`) sit outside the marker zone — they are proof plumbing, not statement content.

**Biconditional `↔` rather than two split theorems.** The LN's "iff" lands as a single `Iff`. Downstream consumers project `.mp` (acyclic $\Rightarrow$ existence — the workhorse for chapter-4+ ADMG traversals, since `IsADMG` carries `IsAcyclic` as its first conjunct) or `.mpr` (the certificate route for causal-discovery algorithms) at zero syntactic cost. Bundling keeps the cross-reference grep-able under one name.

**Existential `∃ lt : Node → Node → Prop, G.IsTopologicalOrder lt` over a bare relation.** Forced by `def_3_8`'s shape. Four bundled alternatives are rejected. A canonical-pick function — a CDMG admits many topological orders; the LN never picks one. `∃ lt : LinearOrder Node, …` — forces totality on the full `Node` type, excluding orders that leave nodes outside $J \cup V$ unrelated. `Nonempty` or `Σ`-bundle — inject uniqueness or an extractable canonical witness. Subtype-restricted `↥(G.J ∪ G.V)` — forces consumer coercions at every use site. The bare relation is the right semantic shape; `Classical.choice` is the downstream API for fixing one.

**Nested 2-conjunct `IsTotalOrder lt ∧ parent-precedence` inherited from `def_3_8`.** The three strict-total-order axioms are packaged into a named sub-predicate so downstream rows like `def_3_9`'s `Pred` carry `IsTotalOrder` as an explicit hypothesis without dragging parent-precedence along. The proof's `refine ⟨fun u v => s u v ∧ u ≠ v, ⟨?_, ?_, ?_⟩, ?_⟩` and `rintro ⟨lt, ⟨hi, htr, htri⟩, hp⟩` destructure this shape directly.

**Proof strategy: walk-reachability partial order plus Szpilrajn.** Define $\mathrm{le}_0\,u\,v := u = v \lor \exists\,p, p.\text{IsDirectedWalk} \land p.\text{length} \ge 1$. Reflexivity is trivial, transitivity is walk concatenation (`Walk.comp`), antisymmetry is acyclicity. After `haveI` registers `IsPreorder` and `IsPartialOrder`, Mathlib's `extend_partialOrder` (Szpilrajn) yields a linear order `s`; reading `lt u v := s u v ∧ u ≠ v` discharges every `IsTopologicalOrder` axiom. The backward direction routes through `Walk.lt_of_directedWalk_pos`, walking along directed edges using parent-precedes plus transitivity to force `lt v v` from a hypothetical self-loop. A hand-rolled `Finset` recursion on parent-less picks (matching the LN's enumeration) is considered and rejected: Szpilrajn already proves the abstract content.

**Typed `WalkStep` constructors drive directed-walk destructuring.** Helpers pattern-match on the constructor: `.forwardE h` exposes `h : (u, v) ∈ G.E` directly; `.backwardE _` and `.bidir _` close by `hp.elim` since their `IsDirectedWalk` is `False` definitionally. The channel is structurally preserved — an L-step cannot masquerade as a directed E-step.

**Surface signature minimal; labels `L` absent from both sides.** Only `(G : CDMG Node)` is hypothesised. `[Fintype Node]` is considered (a constructive variant could enumerate `(G.J ∪ G.V).toList`) and rejected: the classical Szpilrajn route never enumerates. Neither `G.IsAcyclic` nor `G.IsTopologicalOrder` reads `G.L`; this is a property of the $(J, V, E)$-skeleton alone, and the `Finset (Sym2 Node)` typing of `G.L` from `def_3_1` does not propagate. The `∃` deliberately does not designate a canonical topological order — downstream consumers needing a specific order pass `lt` as an explicit hypothesis or `Classical.choice`-extract from the existential.
